title-group.article-title: Metabolic drivers of genome instability in cancer: mechanisms and therapeutic opportunities

abstract.p: Metabolic reprogramming and genome instability represent two fundamental hallmarks of cancer. Emerging studies now demonstrate that specific metabolic alterations directly fuel replication stress, DNA damage, and compromised DNA damage response. This underscores that metabolites are not merely passive by-products but active biochemical regulators of genome instability. Perturbation of specific metabolic pathways can preferentially unmask these vulnerabilities for therapeutic targeting. In this review, we propose an integrated framework highlighting metabolism-induced genome instability as a potential therapeutic target. By delineating the metabolic targets that induce genome instability, we provide a comprehensive overview of the complex interplay between metabolic pathways and genome stability. We further highlight that metabolism-induced genome instability can be strategically exploited to potentiate standard-of-care therapies. Collectively, these insights redefine metabolism-induced genome instability as a targetable vulnerability of cancer. This systematic synthesis provides a mechanistic rationale for next-generation therapeutic designs in which metabolic interventions are leveraged to convert genome instability into actionable clinical vulnerabilities.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Metabolic reprogramming and genome instability are two established hallmarks of cancer that are mechanistically interlinked [1, 2]. Metabolic reprogramming endows cancer cells with the metabolic plasticity to rewire pathways for bioenergetic and redox homeostasis [3]. These metabolic shifts, by altering intracellular pools of metabolites, provide the molecular substrates for malignant cells to proliferate [4–6]. In parallel, genome instability renders cancer cells more susceptible to mutations and various forms of DNA damage [7]. It fosters tumor evolution and therapeutic resistance at the cost of increasing genomic fragility [8, 9]. This inherent vulnerability constitutes a fundamental characteristic of cancer, prompting the development of therapeutic strategies that target genome instability.

As early as 1946, Louis Goodman reported the antitumor effects of nitrogen mustard (mechlorethamine) via its DNA alkylating mechanism [10]. Since then, several other agents targeting genome instability have been approved for clinical use, including platinum-based cisplatin [11], topoisomerase inhibitors such as camptothecin and anthracyclines [12], and Poly(ADP-ribose) polymerase (PARP) inhibitors such as olaparib [13]. However, cancer cells often develop resistance to these therapies, limiting their long-term efficacy [14–16]. The emergence of resistance underscores the urgent need to explore adjacent vulnerabilities, particularly within the interconnected realms of metabolism and genome integrity.

Notably, accumulating evidence now demonstrates that cancer-associated metabolic changes are not merely supportive but can directly induce genome instability at multiple levels. For example, oncometabolites such as 2-hydroxyglutarate (2HG), fumarate, and succinate can inhibit α-ketoglutarate (α-KG)–dependent dioxygenases, resulting in aberrant hypermethylation of histone 3 lysine 9 (H3K9) and subsequent impairment of DNA repair fidelity [17, 18]. Similarly, dysregulation of nucleotide and one-carbon metabolism can induce replication stress and promote mutagenesis [19, 20], whereas disturbances in redox homeostasis elevate reactive oxygen species (ROS) levels to induce DNA damage [21]. Integrating these emerging yet scattered observations, we propose a therapeutic strategy that exploits metabolic vulnerabilities to exacerbate genome instability. In this context, metabolic modulators can sensitize cancer cells to PARP inhibitors or genotoxic treatments, offering a promising strategy to overcome therapeutic resistance.

This review delineates how key metabolic pathways drive distinct forms of genome instability in cancer (Fig. 1). Building on these mechanistic insights, we further explore the potential of harnessing metabolite-induced vulnerabilities to transform genome instability into actionable therapeutic targets.

caption.p: Targeting metabolites to induce genome instability. The figure illustrates how five major metabolic aspects induce genome instability through replication stress, DNA damage, and defects in the DNA damage response (DDR). 2HG, 2-hydroxyglutarate; AcH, acetaldehyde; ALDH, aldehyde dehydrogenase; DHFR, dihydrofolate reductase; DHODH, dihydroorotate dehydrogenase; ETC, electron transport chain; FA, formaldehyde; FAO, fatty acid oxidation; FH, fumarate hydratase; G6PD, glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase; Glo1, glyoxalase 1; GPX4, glutathione peroxidase 4; GR, glutathione reductase; GSH, glutathione; HNE, 4-hydroxy-2-nonenal; IDH, isocitrate dehydrogenase; IMPDH, inosine monophosphate dehydrogenase; Kcr, lysine crotonylation; KDM4B, lysine demethylase 4B; Ksuc, lysine succinylation; MDA, malondialdehyde; MG, methylglyoxal; MRN, MRE11-RAD50-NBS1 complex; MTX, methotrexate; NAMPT, nicotinamide phosphoribosyltransferase; NRF2, nuclear factor erythroid 2-related factor 2; OXPHOS, oxidative phosphorylation; PPP, pentose phosphate pathway; PTMs, post-translational modifications; RNR, ribonucleotide reductase; SDH, succinate dehydrogenase; TrxR, thioredoxin reductase; TS, thymidylate synthase

sec.title: Genome instability: classification and mechanisms
sec.p: Genome instability can be broadly classified into three major categories: nucleotide instability (NIN), chromosomal instability (CIN), and microsatellite instability (MSI) [22]. NIN manifests as an increased frequency of point mutations, as well as insertions or deletions involving one or several nucleotides, arising primarily from DNA polymerase misincorporation. CIN encompasses the frequent gain, loss, or structural aberrations of chromosomes, leading to aneuploidy and karyotypic heterogeneity. MSI is characterized by length alterations in repetitive DNA sequences, typically resulting from defects in the mismatch repair (MMR) system [22, 23]. Although these three distinct forms coexist, the metabolic perturbations discussed in this review predominantly induce genome instability that largely manifests as CIN. Therefore, the ensuing discussion primarily focuses on CIN, specifically examining how these metabolic vulnerabilities drive DNA damage accumulation, exacerbate replication stress, and compromise specific DNA repair pathways.

sec.title: DNA damage
sec.p: DNA damage constitutes a fundamental driver of genome instability. Exogenous DNA lesions can directly arise from environmental hazards, including chemical agents, ionizing radiation, and toxic heavy metals. Endogenous sources, such as ROS and metabolic by-products, can also threaten genome stability [24, 25]. These genotoxic insults can induce a wide spectrum of DNA lesions, including single-strand breaks, double-strand breaks (DSBs), base modifications, DNA cross-links, and clustered damage sites. It is estimated that a typical mammalian cell experiences 10,000 to 100,000 endogenous DNA lesions per day (averaging roughly 70,000), most of which are SSBs [26]. If left unrepaired, SSBs can be converted into DSBs, which are considerably more deleterious, as they can lead to oncogenesis or cell death [26–28]. Importantly, the cytotoxic potency of DSBs has been exploited in cancer therapy: many chemotherapeutic agents and radiotherapy induce DSBs in rapidly proliferating cancer cells, thereby preferentially triggering cell death [29].

sec.title: Replication stress
sec.p: Beyond direct DNA damage, replication stress functions as another driver of genome instability. It is defined as the slowing or stalling of replication fork progression due to persistent obstacles [30]. These obstacles stem from diverse sources, including aberrant oncogene activation, nucleotide pool insufficiency, transcription–replication conflicts, and hard-to-replicate DNA regions. If left unresolved, stalled or collapsed replication forks can lead to DNA double-strand breaks and copy number variations, ultimately contributing to CIN [31].

sec.title: Defects in DNA damage response
sec.p: As a cellular safeguard, the DDR constitutes an intricate signaling network that maintains genome stability by detecting DNA lesions and orchestrating cellular fates, including DNA repair, cell cycle arrest, or apoptosis [32]. Although outcomes like apoptosis preclude instability by eliminating irreparably damaged cells, genome instability is primarily driven by defects in the specific execution phases of DNA repair [9]. When critical repair mechanisms falter and cells bypass apoptotic clearance, they persist and replicate with unresolved lesions [9]. To prevent this, sensor proteins such as Ku70/Ku80 detect lesions and trigger the activation of apical kinases (e.g., ATM/DNA-PK). This signaling cascade leads to the downstream phosphorylation of H2AX. As a critical chromatin mark, the resulting γH2AX scaffolds mediators like p53-binding protein 1 (53BP1) to amplify the damage signal and recruit repair factors, thereby ensuring the precise coordination of the DDR [27]. However, disruption of this signaling cascade severely compromises genome integrity. Mutations in checkpoint kinases such as ataxia telangiectasia mutated (ATM), ataxia telangiectasia and Rad3-related (ATR), CHK1, and CHK2 compromise the ability of cells to detect and respond to DNA lesions. Specifically, loss of ATM or CHK2 impairs the recognition and signaling of DSBs, while defects in ATR–CHK1 signaling compromise replication fork stability and checkpoint control, thereby exacerbating replication stress [29, 33–35]. Moreover, defects in DNA repair proteins further contribute to genome instability. Key factors in homologous recombination (HR), including breast cancer type 1/2 susceptibility protein (BRCA1/2) and RAD51, as well as components of the Fanconi anemia (FA) pathway, are essential for faithful DSB repair [36–38]. The mechanistic details of other repair systems have been extensively reviewed elsewhere [27, 39, 40] and will not be reiterated here. Instead, this review focuses on the metabolic-genomic interface, highlighting how these interactions create therapeutic vulnerabilities.

sec.title: Metabolism-induced genome instability: turning instability into vulnerability
sec.p: Emerging studies now demonstrate that altered metabolism can profoundly affect genome stability. If metabolism can be harnessed to actively induce genome instability, this may further exacerbate genome defects and ultimately drive cell death. In this section, we categorize these metabolic targets into major classes and illustrate how each drives genome instability and creates therapeutic vulnerabilities.

sec.title: Disrupting nucleotide pools to induce replication stress
sec.p: Nucleotide metabolism is intimately linked to genome stability because proliferating cells must continuously synthesize deoxyribonucleotides (dNTPs) to support DNA replication [41]. Disruption of nucleotide supply consequently exerts a direct and detrimental impact on replication machinery, resulting in replication stress and NIN [42]. Cellular nucleotide supply is maintained through two main pathways: de novo synthesis and salvage pathways. Several key nodes within nucleotide metabolism have been exploited as therapeutic targets, giving rise to strategies that interfere with nucleotide availability. These approaches include competitive inhibition via nucleotide analogs, blockade of precursors or biosynthetic enzymes, and disruption of folate and broader amino acid-driven metabolic networks [43].

Nucleotide analogs function as structural mimics of natural nucleotides. Upon incorporation into DNA, they disrupt polymerase activity and induce chain termination or base mispairing, ultimately leading to replication stress and apoptosis [44]. This mechanism preferentially targets rapidly proliferating cancer cells. Several purine and pyrimidine analogs, including 5-fluorouracil (5-FU), gemcitabine, 6-mercaptopurine and capecitabine, have been clinically established in oncology [45–48]. In addition, key enzymes responsible for nucleotide biosynthesis, including ribonucleotide reductase (RNR), thymidylate synthase (TS), inosine monophosphate dehydrogenase (IMPDH) and dihydroorotate dehydrogenase (DHODH), are critical for maintaining adequate nucleotide pools [19]. Given their central role in DNA synthesis and repair, targeting these dNTP metabolic enzymes has long represented a successful pharmacological paradigm for cancer therapy [41] (Table 1). Another prominent metabolic target is folate metabolism, which supplies one-carbon units for de novo nucleotide synthesis. Methotrexate (historically known as amethopterin) is a folate analog that competitively inhibits dihydrofolate reductase (DHFR), blocking the conversion of folate to dihydrofolate and tetrahydrofolate [49]. This limits the availability of one-carbon units necessary for nucleotide biosynthesis, thereby impairing DNA synthesis and inducing genome instability [50]. These inhibitors targeting nucleotide metabolism have been extensively evaluated in preclinical and clinical settings; a comprehensive summary of these agents is provided in Table 1.

Beyond the folate cycle, the broader one-carbon metabolism is fundamentally fueled by serine, which serves as the primary carbon donor for de novo purine and thymidylate biosynthesis [51]. Restricting serine availability or targeting synthesis enzymes like PHGDH directly depletes dNTP pools [51]. Although small-molecule PHGDH inhibitors effectively suppress tumor proliferation in preclinical models [52, 53], their therapeutic index may be limited by the essential physiological role of de novo serine synthesis in the central nervous system [54]. Intertwined with this network is the methionine cycle, which generates S-adenosylmethionine (SAM) [55]. SAM acts as the universal methyl donor, and disrupting its synthesis may compromise the epigenetic landscape required for replication fork stability [56]. In addition, while one-carbon metabolism supplies carbons, glutamine anaplerosis acts as the obligate nitrogen donor for assembling purine and pyrimidine rings [57]. Pharmacological inhibition of glutamine metabolism starves cells of nitrogen, abruptly stalling nucleotide synthesis [58]. Notably, although targeting these amino acid dependencies offers promising synthetic lethal vulnerabilities, their corresponding pharmacological inhibitors remain largely in preclinical or early-phase clinical trials, lacking the maturity and broad clinical utility of classic antimetabolites.

Collectively, targeting nucleotide metabolism primarily induces severe replication stress, and current antimetabolites provide effective antitumor strategies. However, because core nucleotide metabolism is inextricably shared with normal proliferative tissues, these agents are historically toxicity-limited. Their narrow therapeutic index underscores the necessity to leverage specific targets rather than relying on broad-spectrum metabolic inhibition. For instance, aneuploidy intrinsically exhausts cellular nucleotide pools, rendering these cancer cells exquisitely sensitive to inhibitors of de novo nucleotide synthesis and thereby establishing a targetable therapeutic window [59].

caption.p: Antimetabolite drugs targeting genome instability in cancer

td.p: λ Inhibits IMPDH and depletes GTP/dGTP pools, inducing replication stress.
λ Competitively inhibits purine synthesis, leading to dNTP imbalance and replication stress
λ Inhibits TS and disrupts dUMP to dTMP conversion, leading to replication stress
λ Inhibits DHODH to block de novo pyrimidine synthesis, inducing replication stress

tr.td: λ Inhibits RNR and depletes dNTP pools, inducing replication stress

td.p: λ Blocks THF regeneration, thereby disrupting nucleotide biosynthesis
λ Acts as a multi-target antifolate to inhibit TS and GARFT, blocking purine and pyrimidine synthesis.

sec.title: Targeting glucose metabolism to induce genome instability
sec.p: Glucose metabolism constitutes a cornerstone of cancer cell bioenergetics and biosynthesis. Cancer cells undergo metabolic reprogramming characterized by enhanced glycolysis, excessive lactate accumulation and activation of the pentose phosphate pathway (PPP), which collectively sustain homeostasis and fuel DNA repair and replication [51]. In this context, targeting glucose metabolism represents a promising strategy to induce genome instability, shifting the metabolic landscape from adaptation toward vulnerability (Fig. 2).

sec.title: Lactylation
sec.p: The aberrant accumulation of lactate in tumors is actively driven by oncogenic events, such as Aurora-A-mediated phosphorylation of LDHB [66]. This accumulated lactate transcends its role as a metabolic byproduct, functioning as a substrate for lactylation that directly modulates DNA repair [67]. Central to this repair mechanism is the MRN complex (MRE11-RAD50-NBS1), a key sensor and transducer of DSBs that recruits ATM kinase to initiate DDR signaling. Recent studies have revealed that both NBS1 and MRE11 undergo lysine lactylation, thereby enhancing HR efficiency [68, 69]. Specifically, TIP60-mediated lactylation of NBS1 at lysine 338 promotes MRN complex assembly and recruitment to DSBs [68], whereas CBP-mediated lactylation of MRE11 at lysine 673 enhances its binding to DNA, facilitating DNA end resection and HR [69]. Importantly, pharmacologic inhibition of lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) or the relevant acetyltransferases abrogates lactylation of MRN components, compromises HR-mediated repair, and drives CIN [69]. Besides MRE11 and NBS1, RAD51 undergoes lactylation at K73 and K40, while H3K9 lactylation drives transcriptional upregulation of RAD51 and BRCA2, collectively enhancing HR repair [70]. Notably, H3K9la and RAD51K73la share the same upstream regulator GCN5, which represents a potential target [70]. These findings establish a direct link between lactylation and genome stability, positioning lactylation as a crucial regulator of tumor DNA repair and a potential metabolic vulnerability.

In contrast to classical metabolic targets validated by multiple independent studies and extensive clinical data, the regulatory role of lactylation in the DDR is a nascent frontier. Currently, these mechanisms are supported by a small number of recent foundational reports [68–70]. Further investigations across diverse tumor types are required to confirm their broad applicability.

sec.title: TCA cycle and oncometabolites
sec.p: Tricarboxylic acid (TCA) metabolites are closely linked to genome stability. In a highly oncogenotype-dependent manner, mutations in TCA enzymes, including isocitrate dehydrogenase (IDH1/2), fumarate hydratase (FH) and succinate dehydrogenase (SDH), lead to the accumulation of the oncometabolites 2HG, fumarate, and succinate respectively [71]. Mechanistically, these TCA-cycle intermediates suppress KDM4B activity, resulting in aberrant hypermethylation of H3K9 at chromatin domains adjacent to DSBs [18]. This hypermethylated state obscures the local H3K9me3 signal necessary for the recruitment of HR factors, thereby impairing DNA repair and inducing CIN [18]. These specific metabolic vulnerabilities are not ubiquitous pan-cancer traits, but are strictly confined to tumors harboring these distinct genetic backgrounds. Beyond oncometabolites, acetyl-CoA promotes HR through histone acetylation at DSB sites [72]. This acetylation is essential for recruiting BRCA1 and restricting the localization of the NHEJ sensor 53BP1 [72, 73]. Therapeutic strategies aimed at modulating acetyl-CoA availability represent a promising approach to manipulate genome stability. ATP-citrate lyase (ACLY), which converts mitochondrial citrate into acetyl-CoA, sustains histone acetylation and BRCA1 recruitment at DSBs [74]. Upon DNA damage, nuclear ACLY undergoes phosphorylation and facilitates histone acetylation to promote HR [72]. Conversely, ACLY inhibition reduces nuclear acetyl-CoA and impairs HR, thereby inducing CIN. In addition, pyruvate, a key glycolytic metabolite generated by pyruvate kinase muscle 2 isoform (PKM2), directly supports the DDR by facilitating γ-H2AX loading through the FACT (facilitates chromatin transcription) complex, thereby promoting DNA repair efficiency and conferring radioresistance [75]. Moreover, pyruvate-derived flux into glutathione biosynthesis provides antioxidant protection against DNA damage caused by oxidative stress [76]. Taken together, these findings highlight the TCA cycle as a pivotal metabolic node for disrupting genome stability.

sec.title: Pentose phosphate pathway
sec.p: The PPP is characteristically upregulated in cancer cells [77]. It bolsters redox homeostasis and nucleotide biosynthesis by supplying nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADPH) and ribose-5-phosphate (R5P), both of which are essential for the maintenance of genome integrity [77]. More specifically, NADPH, generated through the oxidative branch by glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD), fuels antioxidant systems to prevent ROS-induced DNA damage [78]. Notably, recent evidence highlights that aneuploid cancer cells depend on a mitotic G6PD-driven NADPH surge to neutralize ROS and ensure faithful chromosome segregation, and blocking this precipitates chromosomal instability [79]. In parallel, R5P from the non-oxidative branch via transketolase (TKT) provides precursors for nucleotide synthesis, supporting DNA replication and repair [78]. Therapeutically, targeting these key PPP enzymes has emerged as a strategy to induce genome instability. G6PD inhibitors can reduce intracellular glutathione (GSH) levels, elevate ROS levels, and thereby promote autophagy [80, 81]. Inhibition of TKT impairs DNA repair and promotes DNA damage [78]. In summary, the PPP acts as a metabolic determinant of genome instability. Although its dysregulation provides metabolites that favor cancer progression, it also positions the pathway as a promising target for metabolic intervention in cancer.

In conclusion, targeting glucose metabolism impairs the DDR by disrupting lactate- and acetyl-CoA-mediated recruitment of repair complexes and inducing oncometabolite-driven H3K9 hypermethylation. It also depletes PPP-derived precursors essential for DNA synthesis and redox homeostasis. However, the efficacy of glucose metabolism inhibitors is highly assay-dependent. While they trigger genome instability in nutrient-rich in vitro cultures, these effects are frequently blunted in vivo, as the nutrient-deprived tumor microenvironment enables cancer cells to utilize alternative fuels to resolve replication stress.

caption.p: The crosstalk between glucose metabolism and genome instability. Metabolites generated from glycolysis and the TCA cycle exert regulatory effects on the nuclear genome. Lactate accumulation promotes the lactylation of key DNA repair proteins. TIP60 and CREB-binding protein (CBP) mediate the lactylation of NBS1 and MRE11 respectively, facilitating MRN complex (MRE11-RAD50-NBS1) assembly and recruitment of repair proteins to DSBs. Targeting lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) or these acetyltransferases impairs homologous recombination (HR). ATP-citrate lyase (ACLY) converts citrate into acetyl-CoA and promotes histone acetylation to recruit BRCA1. TIP60 acetylates and activates ATM, which subsequently phosphorylates ACLY and CBP in response to DNA damage. Mutations in isocitrate dehydrogenase (IDH), fumarate hydratase (FH), or succinate dehydrogenase (SDH) lead to the accumulation of oncometabolites such as 2-hydroxyglutarate (2HG), fumarate, and succinate, which inhibit KDM4B activity. This results in aberrant H3K9 hypermethylation that blocks the recruitment of HR factors. The pentose phosphate pathway (PPP) supports genome stability by generating NADPH via glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) for antioxidant defense and ribose-5-phosphate (R5P) for nucleotide synthesis

sec.title: Disrupting redox homeostasis to cause oxidative DNA damage
sec.p: Cancer cells reprogram their metabolism to accommodate unchecked proliferation under elevated oxidative burden. A hallmark of this metabolic reprogramming is an enhanced antioxidant capacity, including upregulated redox-sensing proteins, enhanced PPP and increased antioxidant molecules [5]. Conversely, this adaptation also creates specific “redox vulnerabilities” that can be therapeutically exploited. Figure 3 illustrates how targeting these vulnerabilities can induce oxidative DNA damage.

Central to cellular redox sensing is the KEAP1–NRF2 axis. Mechanistically, KEAP1 constitutively mediates NRF2 ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation, whereas oxidative modification of KEAP1 cysteines enables NRF2 stabilization, nuclear translocation, and the consequent activation of antioxidant genes [82]. By coupling metabolic status to transcriptional control of antioxidant programs, the KEAP1–NRF2 pathway maintains redox homeostasis. Dysregulation of this axis unveils redox vulnerabilities and offers a promising therapeutic strategy [83]. In parallel, the thioredoxin (Trx) and glutaredoxin (Grx) systems constitute the principal thiol-disulfide reducing pathways that maintain protein cysteines in a reduced state and directly modulate the activity of DNA repair factors [84]. Trx, reduced by thioredoxin reductase (TrxR) using NADPH as an electron donor, regulates disulfide bonds in repair proteins and signaling kinases [85]. Pharmacologic inhibition of TrxR collapses redox homeostasis and impairs DNA repair capacity [86]. Similarly, the Grx system uses GSH to regulate reversible protein glutathionylation, while GSH itself is regenerated from oxidized GSSG by glutathione reductase (GR) via utilizing NADPH [87].

Targeting key antioxidant components in the Trx and Grx systems can induce genome instability. NADPH, primarily generated by G6PD in the PPP, supports both redox repair systems. Inhibition of G6PD reduces NADPH availability and upregulates ROS levels, suppressing Trx- and Grx-mediated genome protection [88, 89]. Moreover, ATM has been shown to promote G6PD activity and its inhibition compromises antioxidant responses [90, 91]. The synthesis of NADPH also depends on NAD+ through NAD+ kinase (NADK), while NAD+ production is regulated by nicotinate phosphoribosyltransferase (NAPRT) and nicotinamide phosphoribosyltransferase (NAMPT) [92, 93]. Inhibition of these enzymes can induce oxidative stress and represents a promising therapeutic approach [93–95]. GSH is another principal antioxidant molecule that protects DNA from oxidative damage. Cancer cells exploit de novo GSH synthesis and glutathione peroxidase 4 (GPX4) activity to neutralize ROS [96]. Furthermore, amino acid metabolism is inextricably linked to this redox buffering. Glutamine anaplerosis supplies glutamate, while the transsulfuration pathway provides cysteine—the rate-limiting precursor for GSH biosynthesis [57, 97]. Consequently, disrupting glutamine metabolism or the transsulfuration axis directly depletes GSH pools, severely impairing ROS clearance and amplifying oxidative DNA damage [57, 97]. Moreover, depleting GSH through cystine/glutamate antiporter inhibitors or blocking its regeneration with GPX4 inhibitors exposes cancers to lethal ROS accumulation and ferroptosis (a type of cell death caused by ferroptotic lipid peroxidation) [98].

Importantly, within the complex network of redox regulation, NAD+ possesses a dual identity: it is essential for generating NADPH to sustain antioxidant defense systems, and it simultaneously serves as the obligate substrate for PARPs, which are critical sensors and effectors of DNA damage [99, 100]. This dual role creates a profound functional interdependency. Upon extensive DNA damage, hyperactivation of PARP rapidly depletes cellular NAD+ pools, thereby compromising the cell’s antioxidant buffering capacity [100]. Conversely, targeting the NAD+ salvage pathway via NAMPT inhibitors not only exacerbates oxidative stress but directly starves PARP of its requisite substrate [101]. This metabolic-DDR crosstalk explains the mechanistic rationale behind the synthetic lethality observed when NAMPT inhibitors are combined with PARP inhibitors, highlighting NAD+ metabolism as a key therapeutic vulnerability [100].

The mitochondrion is a major intracellular source of ROS, producing superoxide and hydrogen peroxide as metabolic byproducts [102]. Inhibition of the electron transport chain elevates ROS, induces DNA damage and triggers ferroptosis [103]. Metformin, a classical metabolic agent targeting complex I, has been studied clinically for cancer treatment [104–106]. Other complex I inhibitors such as IACS-010759, BAY-87-2243 and MS-L6 show potent anticancer effects in preclinical models [107–110]. However, the clinical development of these drugs has been hampered by a narrow therapeutic index and dose-limiting toxicities, including hyperlactatemia and neurotoxicity [111].
Collectively, impairing antioxidant defenses or increasing ROS can convert metabolic adaptation into a therapeutically exploitable vulnerability.

caption.p: Targeting redox homeostasis to induce oxidative DNA damage. A The mitochondrion is a major intracellular source of ROS. Targeting complex I with inhibitors blocks electron transport, leading to superoxide accumulation. B Exogenous ROS originate from radiation, chemicals, pollutants and dietary factors. C Under basal conditions, KEAP1 promotes NRF2 degradation. Oxidative stress triggers NRF2 release and nuclear translocation to activate antioxidant transcription. The inhibitor brusatol suppresses NRF2 activity, preventing this adaptive response. In glutaredoxin (Grx) system, Xc⁻ imports cystine for Glutathione (GSH) synthesis, while glutamate-cysteine ligase (GCL) controls the rate-limiting step of GSH synthesis. Glutathione reductase (GR) further reduces GSSG to GSH using NADPH. Inhibition of Xc⁻ by Class I ferroptosis inducers (FINs) or inhibition of glutathione peroxidase 4 (GPX4) activity by Class II FINs fails to neutralize lipid peroxides (PUFA-OOH), thereby triggering ferroptosis. In thioredoxin (Trx) system, electrons from NADPH are transferred through thioredoxin reductase (TrxR) to Trx, which then reduces peroxiredoxin (Prx) to facilitate the detoxification of hydrogen peroxide. Inhibition of TrxR by auranofin or Trx by PX-12 impairs antioxidant defense. Both Grx and Trx systems depend on NADPH supply, which is regulated by NAD kinase (NADK) and glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD)

sec.title: Accumulating toxic aldehydes to cause DNA damage
sec.p: Aldehydes are highly reactive metabolic intermediates that pose a significant threat to genome integrity. Their electrophilic carbonyl group enables them to interact with nucleophilic sites in DNA, leading to the generation of base adducts, intra- and interstrand crosslinks, and DNA–protein crosslinks. Failure to properly repair these lesions can result in genome instability and cell death [112].

Formaldehyde, the simplest aldehyde, is generated endogenously via oxidative demethylation reactions and one-carbon metabolism [113, 114]. It reacts with DNA nucleobases to form hydroxymethyl adducts that induce base substitutions, intrastrand crosslinks, and DNA–protein crosslinks [115]. Acetaldehyde, primarily derived from ethanol metabolism, forms N2-ethylidene-deoxyguanosine adducts. These lesions can induce frameshift deletions that stall replication forks [116]. Methylglyoxal (MG), a glycolytic by-product, forms imidazopurinone adducts on DNA, resulting in depurination and abasic sites. It also modifies histones, thereby disrupting chromatin structure and generating genotoxic histone–DNA crosslinks [116]. In addition, reactive aldehydes, such as 4-hydroxy-2-nonenal (HNE), acrolein and malondialdehyde (MDA), exert cytotoxicity by forming exocyclic DNA adducts, contributing to genome instability [117].

Given their potent genotoxicity, aldehydes represent a double-edged sword in cellular metabolism. They function not only as drivers of DNA damage but also as potential therapeutic targets. The intrinsic cytotoxicity of aldehydes can be therapeutically exploited by inhibiting the metabolic pathways responsible for their detoxification [118]. Central to aldehyde clearance are aldehyde dehydrogenases (ALDHs), whose inhibition results in the accumulation of endogenous aldehydes beyond cellular buffering capacity [112]. Overexpression of ALDHs is observed in most cancers, thereby reducing the toxic effects on cancer cells [119]. Therefore, selective inhibition of ALDH isoforms offers a metabolism-centered strategy to exploit tumor cell susceptibility to aldehyde-induced genome instability [120]. Similarly, Glyoxalase 1 (Glo1), a key enzyme in MG detoxification, is frequently upregulated in cancers [121]. Inhibition of Glo1 could elevate cytotoxic MG levels, inducing apoptosis and replication catastrophe [122]. Taken together, metabolic enzymes emerge as promising targets to induce genome instability and establish therapeutic vulnerability.

sec.title: Targeting lipid metabolism to cause DNA damage and impair the DDR
sec.p: Aberrant lipid metabolism, characterized by upregulated de novo lipogenesis and cholesterol biosynthesis, is required for cancer growth [123]. This metabolic alteration not only provides structural and energetic substrates but also reshapes the cellular lipid landscape in a manner that promotes genome instability [123].

The direct pathway linking dysregulated lipid metabolism to genome instability is lipid peroxidation (LPO), which arises from the oxidative milieu typical of the tumor microenvironment. LPO cleaves polyunsaturated fatty acids into highly reactive electrophilic aldehydes, such as MDA and HNE [124]. These aldehydes subsequently form exocyclic DNA adducts, inducing genome instability [125]. Furthermore, targeting deregulated de novo lipid synthesis has been a potential therapy in glioblastoma (GBM). YTX-7739, a clinical-stage inhibitor of stearoyl-CoA desaturase (SCD), can induce lipotoxicity and impair the DDR, showing therapeutic efficacy in mouse models [126].

Beyond direct action on cancers, lipid metabolism profoundly regulates the DDR through protein post-translational modifications (PTMs). For instance, succinylation of histones, including H3K122 [127] and H4K77 [128], as well as non-histone proteins such as p53 [129] and FEN1 [130], has been shown to modulate chromatin dynamics, replication fork stability and DNA repair efficiency [131]. Similarly, palmitoylation regulates the DDR by modulating the activation of key DDR kinases, the assembly of DNA damage foci, and the recruitment of repair factors such as Rif1 to double-strand breaks [132, 133]. Dysregulation of palmitoylation in cancers, resulting from altered Zinc finger Asp-His-His-Cys-type palmitoyltransferase (zDHHC) expression or epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) signaling, impairs checkpoint activation and compromises genome stability [134]. Other types of fatty acylation, such as crotonylation, are also intimately linked to the DDR [135, 136]. Moreover, fatty acid oxidation (FAO) has been shown to promote DNA repair by supplying acetyl-CoA for PARP1 acetylation [137]. The inhibition of FAO diminishes PARP1 activity, thereby compromising genome stability. Collectively, these findings highlight lipid-related PTMs as critical DDR regulators and potential therapeutic targets in cancer.

In addition, sex steroid hormones have been implicated in the modulation of genome stability in endocrine-driven malignancies, including breast and prostate cancer [138]. For example, estrogen can precipitate the formation of R-loops and DSBs [139, 140], synergistically inducing genome instability in BRCA1-deficient breast cancer [141]. It also exerts a dual effect on DSB repair, either facilitating or impeding repair under different conditions [142]. As new endocrine therapies emerge, dissecting hormone–DDR crosstalk will be key to exploiting genome instability for therapy.

In summary, dysregulated lipid metabolism drives genome instability through a dual mechanism: LPO products directly induce DNA damage via adduct formation, while aberrant lipid-derived PTMs and hormones impair the signaling and execution of the DDR. However, it is crucial to distinguish whether these metabolic alterations are causal drivers of genome instability or merely correlative by-products of a stressed tumor microenvironment. Establishing a definitive causal link requires rigorous mechanistic dissection.

sec.title: Targeting metabolism to sensitize standard therapies
sec.p: Altered metabolism engenders vulnerabilities that can be exploited to enhance the efficacy of conventional therapies, including chemotherapy, radiotherapy and targeted agents [143]. In this section, we summarize how metabolic interventions sensitize cancer cells to these therapies, offering a potential avenue for combination therapy.

sec.title: Targeting glucose metabolism to overcome therapeutic resistance
sec.p: Since glucose metabolism regulates the DDR through both metabolite signaling and enzymatic crosstalk, targeting these metabolic dependencies offers an avenue to compromise cellular defense mechanisms and resensitize cancers to genotoxic treatments (Table 2). Fructose-1,6-bisphosphate (F1,6P), a key glycolytic intermediate, directly binds to High Mobility Group Box 1 (HMGB1) and disrupts its oligomerization [144]. This interaction inhibits the ability of HMGB1 to support DNA repair and sensitizes cancer cells to chemotherapeutic drugs [144]. Moreover, multiple enzymes in glycolysis and the PPP, including Hexokinase 2 (HK2), Glucose Transporter 1 (GLUT1), Phosphofructokinase-2 (PFK2), PKM2, G6PD and TKT, have emerged as promising therapeutic targets. Although these metabolic enzymes do not all directly regulate the DDR through metabolic products, their canonical enzymatic activities are nonetheless pivotal in regulating genome instability. This offers another promising therapeutic avenue to sensitize cancer cells to DNA-damaging agents. For instance, HK2, the first rate-limiting enzyme in glycolysis, localizes to the nucleus, where it interacts with chromatin-associated factors such as 53BP1 and RAD51, potentially influencing the DDR [145]. Therapeutically, targeting HK2 offers a promising strategy to sensitize tumors to DNA-damaging agents. 3-Bromopyruvate (3-BP), a potent glycolytic inhibitor targeting HK2, can potentiate the efficacy of 1,3-bis(2-chloroethyl)-1-nitrosourea (BCNU) and Daunorubicin [146, 147]. By depleting ATP, elevating ROS, and impairing DNA repair capacity, 3-BP amplifies drug-induced interstrand crosslinks and apoptotic signaling, thereby increasing sensitivity [147]. Moreover, 2-deoxy-D-glucose (2-DG), an inhibitor of HK2, improves the therapeutic efficacy of temozolomide and radiotherapy in GBM [148]. Similarly, in non-small cell lung cancer, co-administration of 2-DG with metformin synergistically augments ROS accumulation and DNA adduct formation, thereby inducing apoptosis [149]. Additional mechanistic details of other enzymes are summarized in Table 2.

As discussed in the previous section, cancer cells with TCA mutations and accumulation of oncometabolites manifest a “BRCAness” phenotype. They display heightened sensitivity to PARP inhibition, representing a synthetic lethal vulnerability that has been exploited in multiple studies [150, 151]. However, current clinically approved agents, such as Ivosidenib [152–154], function by reversing the mutated IDH1 phenotype, thereby attenuating the tumor’s acquired sensitivity to PARP inhibition. This paradoxical clinical application warrants closer scrutiny and further investigation. Sulkowski et al. established the rationale for a potential therapeutic strategy by targeting the 2HG-dependent HR deficiency with PARP inhibition, rather than attempting to block 2HG production [71]. Here, we propose that this vulnerability to PARP inhibitors should be leveraged rather than suppressed, offering an alternative therapeutic strategy. Similarly, other combination therapies targeting cancers deficient in FH and SDH are being explored and hold considerable promise [71, 151, 155, 156].

Accumulates ROS, forms DNA adducts and damages DNA

Induces EGFR protein degradation and compromises DNA damage repair, ultimately leading to apoptosis
Hinders RRM2 recruitment and dNTP incorporation, and disrupts the assembly of FA repair factors

Approved denotes FDA approval. “–” indicates that the agent is currently in the preclinical study stage. “Healthy Volunteers” refers to safety and PK trials conducted in non-cancer populations

sec.title: Targeting metabolite-derived PTMs to enhance therapeutic effect
sec.p: PTMs represent a pivotal regulatory layer within the DDR. Emerging evidence has revealed that many of these PTMs are metabolite-derived, directly linking cellular metabolic state to genome stability and therapeutic sensitivity. In this section, we highlight key metabolite-driven modifications that enhance therapeutic efficacy, providing a rationale for the development of combination treatment strategies (Table 3).

sec.title: Lactylation
sec.p: Lactylation enhances DNA repair efficiency, driving resistance to multiple therapies targeting genome instability [68]. A direct approach to counteract this resistance involves combining LDH or LDHA inhibition with genotoxic agents such as cisplatin, PARP inhibitors or ionizing radiation [68, 69, 171]. In addition, pharmacological inhibition of acetyltransferases, including GCN5 and CBP, represents another strategy to potentiate the cytotoxic efficacy of cisplatin and PARP inhibitors [69, 70]. Notably, irinotecan, a topoisomerase inhibitor primarily used as a first-line treatment for colorectal and pancreatic cancer, exhibits synergistic efficacy when combined with anthracyclines [172]. Mechanistically, this is achieved by inhibiting Bloom syndrome protein lactylation and suppressing HR repair in pan-cancer patient-derived xenograft models, highlighting its potential as a combination therapy [172].

sec.title: GDP-M-mediated ubiquitination
sec.p: Accumulation of Guanosine diphosphate–mannose (GDP-M) in triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) has been shown to impair HR by promoting BRCA2 ubiquitination through the disruption of its interaction with USP21. By exploiting this vulnerability, GDP-M supplementation sensitizes tumor cells to DNA-damaging agents, which significantly attenuates tumor growth in vitro and in vivo. Importantly, this combination therapy also augments the efficacy of anti-PD-1 antibodies by activating stimulator of interferon genes (STING)-dependent antitumor immunity. This result highlights GDP-M–mediated HR suppression as a metabolic vulnerability for combination therapy in HR-deficient cancers [173]. Nevertheless, it is important to emphasize that this mechanism is currently supported by a recent foundational report and remains highly context-dependent.

sec.title: Palmitoylation
sec.p: Palmitoylation has emerged as another critical PTM involved in overcoming drug resistance. In non-small-cell lung cancer, site-specific palmitoylation of EGFR dynamically controls receptor stability, membrane localization, and downstream signaling fidelity [174]. Inhibition of this lipid modification by 2-bromopalmitate (2-BP) potentiates the cytotoxicity of EGFR-targeted inhibitors such as gefitinib (Table 3) [175]. Intriguingly, disruption of EGFR palmitoylation not only attenuates receptor activation but also reinforces “oncogenic addiction,” rendering cancer cells hypersensitive to EGFR inhibition [176]. Beyond drug sensitization, palmitoylation also intersects with the DDR, where loss of ZDHHC8 impairs G2/M checkpoint activation, inducing CIN and enhancing radiosensitivity [177].

sec.title: Acetylation
sec.p: Given the fundamental role of acetylation in the DDR, several studies have combined conventional therapies with histone deacetylase (HDAC) inhibitors [178, 179]. Notably, the co-inhibition of PARP and HDAC induces a “BRCAness” phenotype, thereby restoring synthetic lethality and promoting the accumulation of cytosolic DNA [180]. Vorinostat (suberoylanilide hydroxamic acid), a well-characterized HDAC inhibitor, has been shown to synergize with olaparib to induce apoptosis and cell cycle arrest [181, 182]. This combination therapy holds great potential for future clinical trials [183].

sec.title: Methylation
sec.p: Protein methylation plays critical roles in regulating the DDR, making methyltransferases attractive targets for combination cancer therapy [184]. DOT1L, a key H3K79 methyltransferase, facilitates HR-mediated repair of DSBs. Its inhibition by EPZ5676 impairs the DDR, reduces γH2AX signaling, and sensitizes cancer cells to PARP inhibitors and chemotherapy [185, 186]. Similarly, the H3K9 methyltransferase G9a modulates HR by interacting with RPA and Rad51, and its inhibition using UNC0638, BIX-01294, or UNC0642 disrupts the DDR, enhances radiosensitivity, and synergizes with etoposide and PARP inhibitors [187–191]. Moreover, protein arginine methyltransferases (PRMTs), including PRMT1 and PRMT5, directly regulate the DDR by methylating key factors such as BRCA1, MRE11, and 53BP1 [192]. Pharmacological inhibition of PRMTs compromises DNA repair and overcomes resistance to anti-cancer agents such as gemcitabine and PARP inhibitors, as demonstrated in preclinical and early-phase clinical studies [193–196].

sec.title: O-GlcNAcylation
sec.p: O-GlcNAcylation is a reversible PTM catalyzed by O-GlcNAc transferase (OGT), which attaches N-acetylglucosamine (GlcNAc) to serine and threonine residues of proteins [197]. By modulating the activity of DDR-related proteins, such as YTHDC1 [198], RAD18 [199], RRM1 [200] and HMGB1 [201], O-GlcNAcylation can regulate the DDR and affect genome stability, representing a potential therapeutic target for cancer therapy [202]. Inhibition of OGT has been shown to sensitize cells to gemcitabine [200], cisplatin [203] and radiotherapy [204] (Table 3). Moreover, O-GlcNAcylation at Thr457 of DNA polymerase η is essential for translesion DNA synthesis and genome stability, with its loss impairing lesion bypass and sensitizing cells to cisplatin [205]. However, targeting O-GlcNAcylation faces considerable challenges in clinical translation, as its regulation of the DDR is inherently unstable and highly context-dependent. Since current findings rely heavily on specific in vitro cell-line assays, translating them into robust in vivo and patient-derived evidence remains a major hurdle [206]. This highlights the need for more precise therapeutic strategies.

sec.title: Amplifying oxidative damage through combination therapy
sec.p: Given the inherent oxidative vulnerability previously discussed, therapeutic strategies that enhance ROS damage can synergize with standard therapies to potentiate cancer cell death (Fig. 3). One potent strategy is to combine NRF2 inhibitors with cisplatin, which not only elevates intracellular ROS levels but also obscures the cancer cells’ ability to respond to oxidative stress [210–212]. For example, brusatol has been shown to enhance the poly-ubiquitination and degradation of NRF2, thereby promoting the efficacy of chemotherapeutic agents [213]. Another combination therapy is disrupting the Trx and Grx systems alongside conventional therapies. Small-molecule inhibitors of the GSH pathway, such as buthionine sulfoximine (BSO), block γ-glutamylcysteine ligase and GSH biosynthesis, thereby exacerbating the oxidative stress elicited by radiotherapy and cisplatin [214–216]. Similarly, glutathione reductase contributes to drug resistance by maintaining redox homeostasis, and its inhibition restores sensitivity in chemoresistant populations [217]. PX-12, an irreversible inhibitor of thioredoxin-1 (Trx1), sensitizes GBM cells to cisplatin and temozolomide [218]. It also shows a synergistic effect with CHK1 inhibitors by disrupting the deoxynucleotide pool [219]. Likewise, auranofin, initially developed for rheumatoid arthritis, enhances the efficacy of conventional therapies and overcomes resistance by inhibiting TrxR [220]. Studies have combined auranofin with cisplatin and ATR/AKT inhibitors to selectively elevate ROS levels in cancer cells and induce oxidative DNA damage [221–223] (Table 4). In addition, metabolic interventions that restrict the availability of NADPH and NADH further enhance the therapeutic efficacy of oxidative damage. Inhibition of G6PD, NADK or NAMPT compromises the cellular reductive capacity and sensitizes cancer cells to genotoxic therapies [78, 93, 165, 224–226]. Notably, NAMPT inhibitors profoundly disrupt PARP-mediated DNA repair pathways, and exhibit synthetic lethality with PARP inhibitors [227]. However, the success of this strategy is heavily context-dependent, particularly regarding tumor oxygenation status. In hypoxic tumor microenvironments, basal ROS production and antioxidant defense networks are fundamentally altered, which can profoundly limit the efficacy of ROS-amplifying agents compared to observations in well-oxygenated in vitro models [228].

Inducing ferroptotic lipid peroxidation provides an additional axis to amplify oxidative lethality. Classic class I ferroptosis inducers (FINs), including erastin and sulfasalazine, inhibit system Xc⁻ and restore cisplatin sensitivity in resistant cancers [229, 230]. The recently developed imidazole ketone erastin, a metabolically stable analog of erastin, is a potent and selective inhibitor and warrants further investigation in combination with other therapies [231]. Similarly, class II FINs directly inhibit GPX4 and potentiate the efficacy of chemotherapy [232]. Other studies have shown that FINs sensitize radioresistant cancer cells to ionizing radiation by suppressing solute carrier family 7 member 11 (SLC7A11) or GPX4 [233, 234] (Table 4). However, several hurdles impede the clinical translation. A major barrier is the suboptimal pharmacological profile of current GPX4 inhibitors, as GPX4 is essential for normal tissue homeostasis and its inhibition raises toxicity concerns. In addition, multiple immune populations within the tumor microenvironment are also ferroptosis-prone, making it critical to decouple ferroptotic sensitivity between neoplastic cells and anti-tumor immune effectors. Finally, predictive biomarkers that can stratify patients for ferroptosis-inducing therapies remain insufficient. Future work should focus on developing safer ferroptosis inducers with improved therapeutic indices [103, 235].

sec.title: Targeting detoxification enzymes to overcome multidrug resistance
sec.p: Cancer cells rely on detoxification systems to neutralize endogenously generated genotoxic metabolic by-products, thereby shielding their genomes from catastrophic damage [51, 118]. By targeting these defense mechanisms, the cytotoxicity of reactive intermediates can be harnessed to trigger genomic collapse and resensitize resistant tumors to standard therapies (Table 4).

Disulfiram (DSF), a drug long used to treat alcoholism, has recently emerged as a potent anticancer agent through its irreversible inhibition of ALDH1 and ALDH2. This inhibition is mediated by the covalent modification of the catalytic cysteine (Cys302) at the enzyme active site, thereby blocking aldehyde detoxification [236]. Several studies have demonstrated the antitumor activity of DSF, which effectively induces cell death across a broad range of malignancies, including melanoma, breast, pancreatic, GBM and liver cancers [237]. It is also reported that DSF can suppress cancer stem cell proliferation in diverse tumors and preferentially targets BRCA1/2-deficient cells [236, 238]. Importantly, DSF in combination with copper has been shown to effectively enhance the sensitivity of drug-resistant cancer cells to chemotherapeutic agents by targeting ALDH [237, 239]. This complex induces superoxide production and mitochondrial stress, thereby overcoming resistance to both radiation and chemotherapy [240]. Similarly, DSF has been shown to sensitize urothelial carcinoma cells to chemotherapy and enhance radiosensitivity in pancreatic cancer models [241, 242]. In Taxol- and Vincristine-resistant cells, DSF reverses the acquired resistance to microtubule inhibitors [243]. Notably, it has been demonstrated that acetaldehyde specifically inhibits the growth of BRCA1-deficient cancer cells that are resistant to PARP inhibitors, underscoring DSF as a promising candidate for combination therapy [238].

Targeting Glo1 represents another promising anticancer strategy by exploiting the cytotoxic accumulation of MG. Multiple pharmacological inhibitors, including S-p-bromobenzyl-glutathione cyclopentyl diester (BBGC), ethyl pyruvate, TER 117 and curcumin, have been shown to sensitize cancers to chemotherapy [121, 244, 245]. Elevated MG promotes the formation of advanced glycation adducts with DNA and proteins, resulting in genome instability and triggering cell death [122]. Several studies have shown that the accumulation of MG enhances radiosensitivity and chemosensitivity by stimulating ROS production and inducing cell apoptosis [246–248]. Notably, MG exerts these effects preferentially on malignant cells by disrupting glycolysis and mitochondrial respiration with minimal toxicity to normal cells [248].

DNPH1, as a nucleotide sanitizer, can hydrolyze cytotoxic 5-hydroxymethyl-deoxyuridine monophosphate (hmdUMP), thereby preventing its incorporation into DNA. Inhibition of DNPH1 leads to the accumulation of genomic hmdUMP, which serves as a substrate for SMUG1-mediated excision repair, resulting in replication fork collapse, PARP trapping, and apoptotic cell death [249]. Pharmacologic inhibition of DNPH1 restores sensitivity to PARP inhibitors in BRCA-mutant cancers and in castration-resistant prostate cancers [249, 250]. Moreover, this synthetic lethality is particularly pronounced in BRCA-deficient tumors, where HR is already compromised.
Collectively, targeting these detoxification enzymes can overcome therapy resistance and underscores the potential of analogous metabolic enzymes as targets for future combination strategies.

sec.title: Other metabolites synergize with standard therapies
sec.p: Several other metabolic vulnerabilities of cancer cells beyond those addressed in the preceding sections also warrant consideration (Table 4). One illustrative example is estrogen, as estrogen-induced estrogen receptor (ER) hyperactivation induces DNA damage and enhances the therapeutic efficacy of PARP inhibitors [251]. Moreover, polyamines enhance HR-mediated DSB repair by facilitating the DNA strand exchange activity of the RAD51 recombinase [252]. Depletion of polyamines via the ornithine decarboxylase (ODC) inhibitor difluoromethylornithine (DFMO) sensitizes TNBC cells to chemotherapy [253]. Stilbene derivative trans-4,4′-dihydroxystilbene (DHS) downregulates both RAD51 and ribonucleotide reductase M2 (RRM2), synergizing with gemcitabine, cisplatin and PARP inhibitors to suppress cancer development [254, 255]. High-dose ascorbate also sensitizes NSCLC and GBM cells to radiation and chemotherapy [256]. Furthermore, it serves as a practical and well-tolerated agent with preferential toxicity toward cancer cells [256]. In addition, other natural products such as curcumin, shikonin derivatives, vitamin D and pulsatilla saponin D, have been shown to suppress multiple DDR pathways and sensitize cancer cells to PARP inhibitors [257–261]. However, unlike the targeting of classical metabolic pathways, the synergistic effects of these natural products are currently supported by a limited body of evidence. Their specific mechanisms and broad applicability require further extensive validation.

sec.title: Impediments to clinical translation
sec.p: Despite the compelling mechanistic evidence, several critical barriers impede the clinical translation of metabolism-induced genome instability. These barriers span from molecular drug properties and cellular adaptive responses to tissue-level heterogeneity and, ultimately, the clinical therapeutic index (Fig. 4).

sec.title: Pharmacological barriers and druggability
sec.p: At the molecular level, the translation is primarily constrained by technical bottlenecks in drug development. Certain metabolic enzymes, although biologically validated as targets, remain recalcitrant to inhibition due to suboptimal physicochemical profiles for human use. A major hurdle is designing small molecules that competitively antagonize high intracellular concentrations of natural substrates while maintaining sufficient bioavailability and membrane permeability [267]. Furthermore, achieving sustained target engagement within the tumor requires optimized pharmacokinetics to ensure the drug reaches effective concentrations, overcoming issues of stability and rapid clearance.

sec.title: Network plasticity and adaptation
sec.p: At the cellular level, the intrinsic redundancy of metabolic networks fuels adaptive resistance [268]. Cancer cells exhibit remarkable plasticity to rewire their metabolic fluxes by mobilizing alternative biosynthetic precursors or activating salvage pathways [268]. This flexibility allows malignant cells to bypass specific enzymatic blockades and sustain the supply of substrates necessary to resolve replication stress. Consequently, the inhibition of a single metabolic node often fails to induce lethal genome instability. Instead, it exerts selective pressure that drives the emergence of metabolically resilient subpopulations, thereby negating the intended genotoxic impact and leading to disease recurrence.

sec.title: Metabolic heterogeneity and stratification
sec.p: Scaling up to the tissue level, the metabolic landscape of tumors is characterized by profound heterogeneity. Shaped by diverse oncogenic drivers and microenvironmental gradients, distinct subclones may exhibit divergent metabolic dependencies [269]. Particularly, oxygen gradients act as critical determinants of the DDR. In the hypoxic tumor core, hypoxia-mediated transcriptional repression downregulates HR genes, such as BRCA1 and RAD51 [270]. This suppression induces a localized “contextual BRCAness,” whereby hypoxic cells exhibit distinct DDR deficiencies compared to their well-oxygenated counterparts [271]. Consequently, clinical success relies on the validation of robust biomarkers to accurately stratify patients who are biologically predisposed to respond [51, 272]. This highlights the importance of integrating multi-omics profiling with functional metabolic imaging to capture the dynamic metabolic state of the cancer [43, 273].

sec.title: Therapeutic index and selectivity
sec.p: Ultimately, the pervasive nature of core metabolic pathways poses a fundamental challenge to achieving a favorable therapeutic index. Given that core metabolic processes, including nucleotide synthesis, glycolysis, and redox defense, are fundamentally shared with normal proliferative tissues, broad-spectrum metabolic inhibition often lacks true selectivity and precipitates dose-limiting systemic toxicities [43]. However, a viable therapeutic index can emerge in the context of specific genotypes. For example, tumors harboring specific genetic defects, such as HR deficiency, exhibit an exquisite reliance on compensatory metabolic pathways, creating unique synthetic lethal vulnerabilities. Therefore, the overarching goal of clinical translation is not to seek universal metabolic selectivity, but to identify these specific oncogenotypes and delineate the precise metabolic windows where cancer cells are preferentially vulnerable to induced genome instability while normal tissues remain spared [274]. Achieving this selective lethality necessitates a precision oncology paradigm and a rigorous framework for clinical assessment and application.

caption.p: Impediments to clinical translation. A Pharmacological barriers and druggability. Issues with drug bioavailability, and membrane permeability limit intracellular target engagement. B Network plasticity and adaptation. Metabolic rewiring drives colony selection and adaptive resistance. C Metabolic heterogeneity and stratification. Multi-omics profiling and metabolic imaging are required for patient stratification. D Therapeutic index and selectivity. Achieving selective lethality in cancer cells while maintaining homeostasis in normal tissues remains a challenge

sec.title: Conclusions and future perspectives
sec.p: The convergence of metabolic reprogramming and genome instability constitutes a fundamental axis driving tumor evolution, while simultaneously presenting a critical therapeutic liability. As synthesized in this review, cancer cells harness metabolic pathways not merely for bioenergetic support, but as essential regulators that orchestrate DNA repair and maintain genome stability. This intricate dependency reveals that disrupting metabolic homeostatic safeguards transcends mere nutrient deprivation; rather, it actively precipitates catastrophic genome instability. While prior literature has extensively discussed isolated metabolic dependencies, a systematic synthesis linking specific metabolic nodes to distinct forms of genome instability remains largely fragmented. Rather than merely cataloging individual pathways, this review provides a structured taxonomy and a mechanistic rationale for combination strategies.

Moving forward, the clinical translation of these insights requires a profound paradigm shift: transitioning from targeting cancer hallmarks in isolation to exploiting their synthetic lethality. Crucially, future therapeutic designs must conceptualize the crosstalk between metabolism and genome stability as a bidirectional feedback loop. Just as metabolic rewiring drives genome instability, severe genomic aberrations such as aneuploidy intrinsically impose profound metabolic dependencies, exposing cancer cells to unique targeted interventions [275]. Furthermore, realizing this potential demands strategies that address the critical challenges posed by pharmacological barriers, metabolic heterogeneity, network plasticity and dose-limiting toxicities. By integrating multi-omics profiling to identify patient-specific metabolic vulnerabilities, we can preferentially convert the metabolic plasticity of cancers into profound genotoxic lethality.

As we advance towards next-generation therapies, the combination of metabolic modulators with standard-of-care genotoxic agents represents a frontier in overcoming single-agent resistance. By establishing metabolism-induced genome instability as a therapeutic strategy, the review provides a roadmap for clinical innovation and informs therapeutic decision-making. Ultimately, this strategy turns the cancer’s metabolic adaptations into targetable vulnerabilities, paving the way for next-generation therapies designed to overcome resistance and achieve improved clinical outcomes.
